mappings.rationaleAI: Phoenician city-states (Byblos, Sidon, Tyre) with Mediterranean trade, colonization including Carthage, and alphabet invention directly address Canaanite-Phoenician commercial city-states (F.2.a). Carthage's foundation connects to North Africa (I.5).
Alexander's conquest of the Achaemenid Persian Empire (330 BC) and founding of Alexandria directly address the Achaemenid Empire under successors and Greek rule (G.3).

The Achaemenid Empire from Cyrus through Xerxes and subsequent decline directly addresses the Achaemenid Persian Empire section (G.2, G.3), covering satrapy administration, Persepolis, conflicts with Greece, and conquest by Alexander.

Mesopotamia as a cradle of civilization covering Sumerian, Akkadian, Babylonian, and Assyrian civilizations directly addresses Mesopotamia and Iran to c. 1600 BC (B) and Mesopotamia from c. 1600 to c. 900 BC (E.3).
Ancient Egypt's pharaonic rule across Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms directly addresses emergence of river valley civilization in Egypt (C) and the New Kingdom (E.1).

Cyrus II's establishment of rule from Anatolia to eastern Iran, including the Edict of Restoration freeing Jewish exiles, directly addresses Cyrus's role in establishing the Achaemenid Empire (G.2.d.ii).

Sumer as the earliest known civilization with cuneiform writing, city-state governance, and temple economies directly addresses the Sumerians from their origins through the Early Dynastic Period (B.1.b).
Hammurabi's establishment of the Old Babylonian Empire and his law code directly address the establishment of the Old Babylonian Empire under the dynasty of Hammurabi (B.1.d.iii).
The Bronze Age provides the archaeological framework for Mesopotamian (B), Egyptian (C), Syrian-Palestinian-Anatolian-Aegean (D), and New Kingdom-era civilizations (E).
Ancient history covering West Asia, Egypt, and Africa from writing through late antiquity broadly addresses the character and achievements of ancient Near Eastern civilizations (A).
The Iron Age's adoption of smelted iron replacing bronze directly relates to the era of new states of Southwest Asia and the beginning of the Iron Age (F).
African history covering Egypt, Nubia, and Carthage connects to the Nilotic Sudan, South Arabia, Ethiopia, and North Africa (I).
Stone Age cultures in Syria and Palestine from Paleolithic through Neolithic directly address the Paleolithic and Mesolithic periods of the region (D.1.a.i).
The Neolithic origins of farming in Sumer and Egypt connect to development of river valley civilization in Mesopotamia (B.1.a) and agricultural developments in Syria and Palestine (D.1.a).
Rome's penetration into North Africa and destruction of Carthage (146 BC) directly addresses Roman penetration into North Africa and incorporation of native kingdoms (I.5.c).
